mūla.devanāgarī: श्री-भगवान् उवाच लोके ऽस्मिन् द्विविधा निष्ठा पुरा प्रोक्ता मयानघ | ज्ञान-योगेन सांख्यानां कर्म-योगेन योगिनाम्
mūla.iast: śrī-bhagavān uvāca loke 'smin dvividhā niṣṭhā purā proktā mayānagha | jñāna-yogena sāṃkhyānāṃ karma-yogena yoginām
mūla.speaker: Krishna

senses_attested_in_panel.sense: कल्पितः स्यात्
अस्मिन् शास्त्रार्थानुष्ठानाधिकृतानां त्रैवर्णिकानां द्विविधा द्विप्रकारा निष्ठा स्थितिः अनुष्ठेयतात्पर्यं पुरा पूर्वं
विचित्राधिकारिसंपूर्णे द्विविधा निष्ठा ज्ञानकर्मविषया यथाधिकारम् असंकीर्णा
शुद्धाशुद्धान्तःकरणभेदेन द्विविधे जने द्विविधा द्विप्रकारा निष्ठा स्थितिर्ज्ञानपरता कर्मपरता
द्विप्रकारा निष्ठा स्थितिः अनुष्ठेयतात्पर्यं पुरा पूर्वं सर्गादौ प्रजाः सृष्ट्वा तासाम् अभ्युदयनिःश्रेयसप्राप्तिसाधनं व
निष्ठा ज्ञानकर्मविषया यथाधिकारम् असंकीर्णा
अपि जनाः सन्ति गृहस्थादिकर्मत्यागेन ज्ञाननिष्ठाः सनकादिवत् तत्स्था
निष्ठा मया पुरा प्रोक्ता ये तु साङ्ख्यास्तेषां ज्ञानयोगेन सर्वत्यागरूपा स्थितिरुक्ता ये चोक्तयोगाधिकारिणस्तेषामुक्तविधक
निष्ठा मोक्षपरता पुरा पूर्वोध्याये मया सर्वज्ञेन प्रोक्ता स्पष्टमेवोक्ता
द्विप्रकारा निष्ठा स्थितिर्ज्ञानपरता कर्मपरता
स्थितिः अनुष्ठेयतात्पर्यं पुरा पूर्वं सर्गादौ प्रजाः सृष्ट्वा तासाम् अभ्युदयनिःश्रेयसप्राप्तिसाधनं वेदार्थसंप्रदायमाविष
ज्ञानकर्मविषया यथाधिकारम् असंकीर्णा
स्थितिः
मया पुरा प्रोक्ता ये तु साङ्ख्यास्तेषां ज्ञानयोगेन सर्वत्यागरूपा स्थितिरुक्ता ये चोक्तयोगाधिकारिणस्तेषामुक्तविधकर्मयोगे
स्थितिर्ज्ञानपरता कर्मपरता
पूर्वं सर्गादौ प्रजाः सृष्ट्वा तासाम् अभ्युदयनिःश्रेयसप्राप्तिसाधनं वेदार्थसंप्रदायमाविष्कुर्वता प्रोक्ता मया सर्वज्ञेन
उक्तं च सम्यग् अवधृतं त्वया पुरा अपि अस्मिन् लोके विचित्राधिकारिसंपूर्णे द्विविधा निष्ठा ज्ञानकर्मविषया यथाधिकारम् असंक
प्रोक्ता ये तु साङ्ख्यास्तेषां ज्ञानयोगेन सर्वत्यागरूपा स्थितिरुक्ता ये चोक्तयोगाधिकारिणस्तेषामुक्तविधकर्मयोगेनेति
पूर्वोध्याये मया सर्वज्ञेन प्रोक्ता स्पष्टमेवोक्ता
पूर्वाध्याये मया तवात्यन्तहितकारिणा प्रोक्ता प्रकर्षेण स्पष्टत्वलक्षणेनोक्ता
मया सर्वज्ञेन ईश्वरेण
इति निर्देशात्काक्वा
ये तु साङ्ख्यास्तेषां ज्ञानयोगेन सर्वत्यागरूपा स्थितिरुक्ता ये चोक्तयोगाधिकारिणस्तेषामुक्तविधकर्मयोगेनेति
स्पष्टमेवोक्ता
प्रकर्षेण स्पष्टत्वलक्षणेनोक्ता
वा शास्त्रीयमनुष्ठातुमधिक्रियन्ते
वैकमेवाधिकारं मे निश्चित्य वद

advaita.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
advaita.english_rendering: From the beginning of creation I declared two-fold adherence (niṣṭhā) in this world for those qualified by the three castes: the path of knowledge (jñāna-yoga) for the Sāṅkhyas — those who have discriminated Self from non-Self, renounced from brahmacarya itself, and stand established in Brahman alone — and the path of action (karma-yoga) for the yogins still engaged in rites. These two niṣṭhās are not concurrent options for one person; their simultaneous practice is impossible, because they are mutually contradictory (paraspara-virodha). Karma-yoga leads to knowledge-niṣṭhā as its instrumental cause; jñāna-niṣṭhā alone, won through that instrumentality, is the independent means to liberation.
advaita.divergence_note: Śaṅkara explicitly: 'karmaniṣṭhāyā jñānanisṭhāprāptiheuttvena puruṣārthaheuttvam na svātantryeṇa' — karma-niṣṭhā derives its value only as means to jñāna-niṣṭhā, never independently.

viśiṣṭādvaita.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
viśiṣṭādvaita.english_rendering: The Lord declared two coordinate (asaṃkīrṇa) modes of adherence appropriate to the two grades of qualified seekers (adhikārin) that populate this variegated world: jñāna-yoga for the Sāṅkhyas whose minds are already undisturbed, and karma-yoga for the yogins whose intellects are still agitated by sense-objects. Not everyone who has kindled the desire for liberation immediately qualifies for jñāna-yoga; one must first, through desireless action performed as pure worship of the Supreme Person (paramapuruṣārādhana), dissolve the impurities of the mind and render the senses calm. The two niṣṭhās are thus coordinate stages of a single upward arc, neither superior in itself, but each sovereign within its adhikāra.
viśiṣṭādvaita.divergence_note: Rāmānuja: 'asaṃkīrṇa eva mayā uktā' — declared unmixed, each for its own adhikārin. And 18.46 cited: 'svakarmṇā tam abhyarcya siddhiṃ vindati mānavaḥ' — action itself is worship leading to perfection.

dvaita.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
dvaita.english_rendering: Even though knowledge (buddhi) is superior in itself, the Lord here declares that two kinds of persons exist in the world and each has his divinely assigned station: those like Sanaka who, having renounced action by divine will (īśvara-icchayā), abide in jñāna-niṣṭhā alone, and those like Janaka who remain in action yet possess jñāna simultaneously. Arjuna belongs to the Janaka-type — fit for knowledge precisely through action, not through its abandonment. Even those in jñāna-niṣṭhā act when Hari wills it, as the example of Priyavrata shows; the division is therefore not between knowledge and ignorance but between two forms of obedience to the Lord's will.
dvaita.divergence_note: Madhva: 'īśvara-icchayā viniveśita-karmādhikāraḥ' (Bhāg. 5.1.23 cited) — fitness for action is itself installed by the Lord's will.

śuddhādvaita.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
śuddhādvaita.english_rendering: The Lord corrects Arjuna's confusion: the two-fold niṣṭhā he declared earlier is not a rivalry between equal independent paths to liberation, but a gracious distribution according to adhikāra — for the Sāṅkhyas who have renounced everything, jñāna-yoga as complete relinquishment; for those qualified in the karma-yoga described, action in the ordained form. Each seeker is fit only for what is consonant with his own capacity (svādhikārānusāra), so no anxiety about which path to choose is appropriate. The three modes — yoga, jñāna, and bhakti — are each independently capable of yielding the supreme end, and the Lord graciously provides all three.
śuddhādvaita.divergence_note: Vallabha: 'svādhikārānusāreṇaiva sarvaṃ yogyam' — all is fitting only when aligned with one's own adhikāra. And: 'yoga-jñānaṃ ca bhaktiś ca' — all three cited as independent mokṣa-hetus.

bhakti.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
bhakti.english_rendering: Śrīdhara clarifies that the Lord never declared two entirely independent (paraspara-nirapekṣa) means of liberation; rather, He declared one single Brahman-niṣṭhā (eka brahma-niṣṭhā) expressed in two modes according to the purity or impurity of the inner instrument (antaḥkaraṇa). For those of purified mind already standing on the jñāna-bhūmikā, the mode is jñāna-yoga — remaining absorbed in contemplation. For those still ascending toward that platform through purification, the mode is karma-yoga as the means of antaḥkaraṇa-śuddhi. The verse 2.39 — 'eṣā te abhihitā sāṅkhye buddhir yoge tv imāṃ śṛṇu' — is Śrīdhara's own proof that both modes were announced as aspects of one niṣṭhā, not two.
bhakti.divergence_note: Śrīdhara: 'dvābhyām ekā brahma-niṣṭhoktā... guṇapradhānabhūtayos tayoḥ svātantryānupapatteḥ' — the two aspects cannot stand independently because one is subordinate (guṇa) to the other (pradhāna).

advaita-bhakti.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
advaita-bhakti.english_rendering: Madhusūdana confirms that the singular form 'niṣṭhā' is deliberate — there is one niṣṭhā that appears two-fold only by the distinction of stages (bhūmikā-bheda), not two independently valid alternatives. For those of purified antaḥkaraṇa who have reached the jñāna-bhūmikā through renunciation from brahmacarya itself, jñāna-yoga is their form of that one niṣṭhā. For those of impure antaḥkaraṇa not yet ascended to that platform, karma-yoga is their form — its purpose being antaḥkaraṇa-śuddhi, which then opens access to jñāna-niṣṭhā. Neither samuccaya (combination) nor vikalpa (option) between the two is admissible; the sequence is strict. Kṛṣṇa addresses Arjuna as 'anagha' (sinless) to signal his existing fitness for instruction, and to indicate that the Lord's purpose is pure benefit, not confusion.
advaita-bhakti.divergence_note: Madhusūdana: 'ekā niṣṭhā sādhyasādhanāvasthābhedena dviprakārā na tu dve eva svatantre niṣṭhe iti kaṭhayituṃ niṣṭhety ekavacanaṃ' — the grammatical singular is the argument.

corpus_provenance.mūla: Belvalkar critical edition (BORI 1947), via Ambuda multi-witness

so_what_questions: If karma-yoga and jñāna-yoga are sequential stages of a single path (Śaṅkara, Śrīdhara, Madhusūdana), what observable signal tells a practitioner they have completed the first stage and may enter the second — and who adjudicates that transition?

Rāmānuja and Madhva treat both niṣṭhās as coordinate options assigned by adhikāra; how does one determine one's own adhikāra without circular self-assessment, and what prevents the convenient self-assignment to whichever path demands less?

The verse's grammatical singular 'niṣṭhā' (one adherence) is load-bearing for Madhusūdana's entire argument. Does the philological case hold against the schools that read two independent paths, and what does the answer imply for how bhāṣya-reading itself works?

Madhva introduces divine will (īśvara-icchā) as the ultimate allocator of adhikāra. Does this dissolve human agency in path-selection, or redefine it as discernment of one's assigned station — and how does this interact with the Gītā's repeated imperatives to Arjuna?

Vallabha lists yoga, jñāna, and bhakti as three coordinate self-sufficient paths. If all three reach the same end, what principle prevents further proliferation — is every sincere practice a legitimate niṣṭhā, or is the three-fold list a closed canon?

All schools agree that 'Sāṅkhyas' here does not mean the classical Sāṅkhya school of Kapila but denotes a class of practitioners. What determines who counts as a 'Sāṅkhya' in this context — ontological position, psychological state, or institutional status (renunciate vs householder)?

Kṛṣṇa says 'purā proktā mayā' — declared by me of old. All schools must explain what 'old' means (Vedic creation? prior chapters? prior cosmic cycle). Which school's answer is most coherent with the verse's insistence on temporal priority, and what turns on getting this right?

everyday_applications.advaita: A professional who finds their work spiritually hollow should not immediately abandon it for a monastery. Śaṅkara's reading prescribes a preparatory phase: perform every task without personal stake in outcome (niṣkāma), treating it as antaḥkaraṇa-śuddhi in motion. Renunciation is earned, not grabbed — only when the inner instrument is genuinely purified does one qualify for the contemplative life. Premature renunciation is another form of evasion.
everyday_applications.viśiṣṭādvaita: Rāmānuja's coordinate reading means that householder life — work, family, civic duty — is not spiritually inferior to monastic life; it is its own complete form of worship when performed as paramapuruṣārādhana. Every task re-framed as an offering to Bhagavān transforms karma-yoga from moral hygiene into devotional practice. There is no obligation to graduate from it.
everyday_applications.dvaita: Madhva's Janaka-archetype validates engaged leadership: a CEO, a doctor, a judge who acts with complete competence and knowledge yet remains embedded in action is not spiritually compromised. Their adhikāra was assigned by the Lord; to abandon it in search of the Sanaka-path without divine sanction would itself be a transgression. Discernment here means recognising which archetype you have been placed into.
everyday_applications.śuddhādvaita: Vallabha's grace-distribution reading removes the anxious self-audit that Śaṅkara's sequential model can generate. The practitioner need not constantly measure whether they have completed karma-yoga and may now 'advance' to jñāna-yoga. Kṛṣṇa's prasāda places each person on the appropriate path; the task is wholehearted engagement with the path one finds oneself on, not restless migration between them.
everyday_applications.bhakti: Śrīdhara's one-niṣṭhā-two-modes reading cures a common confusion: people who have begun contemplative practice and find it interrupted by life-duties interpret the interruption as spiritual failure. Śrīdhara says the duties ARE the practice at that stage — antaḥkaraṇa-śuddhi is the work, not a detour from the work. The sign of completion is not a dramatic breakthrough but a steady, natural disinclination toward sense-objects.
everyday_applications.advaita-bhakti: Madhusūdana's grammatical argument — one niṣṭhā, two stages — carries a practical corollary: the practitioner should hold both stages in a single vision rather than treating them as alien regimes. Karma-yoga is not abandoned at the jñāna stage; its form changes. The executive who reaches equanimity does not stop making decisions — they make them from a different ground. The grammar of singular niṣṭhā is the grammar of continuous practice.
